story.title: Long-tailed macaques groom for more than hygiene 🐒
story.description: A clip of primates: long tailed macaques grooming each other
story.seo_title: Long-tailed macaques groom for more than hygiene 🐒
story.yt_description: Long-tailed macaques spend hours grooming each other. But it’s not just about clean fur. Source: Pexels
story.script: They trade fur for protection. Every lick removes dirt and ticks, sure, but also builds friendships. Watch the way one macaque’s eyelids droop—that’s trust. That’s why fights start if they skip a session. You ever seen a macaque hold a grudge? Comment below.

story.title: Macaques groom to make friends not just clean
story.description: A clip of primates: playful macaques grooming in lush jungle
story.seo_title: Macaques groom to make friends not just clean
story.yt_description: Watch macaques grooming in the jungle. The surprise? They're not just cleaning each other — they're trading favors. Source: Pexels
story.script: Macaques groom to trade favors. That’s right — every 20 seconds they swap a scratch for later backup. Watch the eyes when one stops early, the other looks up like, 'Hey, not done yet.' That’s why fights start if the favor feels unfair. So next time you see them grooming, think: who’s keeping score. What’s your go-to trade.
